name: Blues
description:
Blues is built on a foundation of call-and-response phrasing, the 12-bar chord progression, and a rawness that keeps it honest. It draws on African musical traditions and the particular hardship of Black life in the American South, where sharecroppers and laborers turned personal suffering into something universal.

The genre emerged in the Mississippi Delta around the turn of the 20th century and spread north with the Great Migration. Robert Johnson, Muddy Waters, and B.B. King each shaped it into something that outlasted any single era. Almost every popular music genre that followed owes something to the blues.

name: Folk
description:
Folk music is the music people made before anyone was recording it. It traveled by ear from one generation to the next, carrying local dialects, labor songs, ballads, and dance tunes. The defining quality is community ownership: folk songs belong to everyone and belong to no one.

The American folk revival of the 1950s and 60s brought it back into focus, with Woody Guthrie, Pete Seeger, and later Bob Dylan treating it as both an art form and a political instrument. That lineage runs directly into country, indie folk, and singer-songwriter traditions still active today.

name: Classical
description:
Classical music in the Western tradition spans roughly 1000 years of composed, notated music, though the Classical period specifically refers to the late 18th century style of Haydn, Mozart, and Beethoven. It is defined by formal structure, harmonic development, and the relationship between composer and performer.

Its influence reaches into film scoring, jazz harmony, progressive rock, and ambient music. The orchestra as an institution, the sonata form, counterpoint, and the idea of music as a serious art form with its own internal logic all trace back here.

name: Jazz
description:
Jazz was born in New Orleans at the intersection of African rhythmic tradition, European harmony, and the blues. It brought improvisation to the center of music-making: a jazz performance is partly composed and partly invented in real time, and the interplay between musicians is the point.

From New Orleans it moved to Chicago, then New York, where bebop in the 1940s pushed it toward greater complexity. Miles Davis alone reshaped the genre multiple times across four decades. Jazz gave the world swing, bebop, modal jazz, fusion, and free jazz, and its vocabulary of harmony and rhythm shaped nearly every genre that followed.

name: Gospel
description:
Gospel music developed in Black American churches as a form of worship that combined African musical traditions with Christian hymns. Call-and-response, emotional intensity, and communal participation are its core features. The music is made to move people, spiritually and physically.

Gospel directly shaped soul, R&B, and rock and roll. Aretha Franklin, Ray Charles, Sam Cooke, and Little Richard all started in gospel before crossing over. The genre gave American popular music its emotional vocabulary.

name: Country
description:
Country music grew out of Appalachian folk traditions, Western swing, and the blues. It tells stories about everyday life in plain language: trucks, heartbreak, small towns, hard work. The guitar, fiddle, and steel guitar are its signature instruments.

The genre was formally established through the Grand Ole Opry in Nashville, and it has cycled through classic country, outlaw country, country pop, and modern bro-country while always returning to its storytelling roots. Hank Williams, Johnny Cash, and Dolly Parton each represent a distinct lineage within it.
key_tracks: I'm So Lonesome I Could Cry

name: Bluegrass
description:
Bluegrass is acoustic string music played fast and clean, with tight vocal harmonies and improvised solos that draw from both country and jazz. Bill Monroe invented it almost single-handedly with his Blue Grass Boys in the mid-1940s, and the style has changed little structurally since then.

The banjo, mandolin, fiddle, dobro, and upright bass are its instruments. Speed and precision matter as much as feeling. The genre stayed stubbornly acoustic while everything around it went electric, which is why it sounds timeless.

name: Rhythm and Blues
description:
Rhythm and blues emerged in the 1940s as urban Black American music with a harder, electrified edge than the Delta blues. It combined the rhythm section of jazz, the feeling of gospel, and the lyrical directness of blues into something made for dancing.

The genre was a direct precursor to rock and roll -- early artists like Little Richard, Fats Domino, and Chuck Berry were being marketed as R&B before the new label caught on. Modern R&B has evolved into something smoother and more pop-influenced, but the original sound set the template for most of what followed.

name: Rock and Roll
description:
Rock and roll named a cultural moment as much as a musical style. It took the energy of R&B, the twang of country, and the electric guitar and put them together for a white teenage audience that had never heard anything quite like it. The backbeat, the distortion, the attitude -- all of it felt dangerous in 1955.

Elvis Presley, Chuck Berry, Little Richard, and Buddy Holly were the architects. Within a decade it had crossed the Atlantic, been reimagined by the Beatles and the Rolling Stones, and set the foundation for the next 70 years of popular music.

name: Soul
description:
Soul music took the gospel church experience and applied it to secular themes. The singing is emotional and full-throated, the arrangements are lush with horns and strings, and the rhythm section swings with intent. It was music that demanded you feel something.

Motown in Detroit and Stax in Memphis developed distinct versions of it through the 1960s. Aretha Franklin, James Brown, and Otis Redding each defined a different aspect of the form. Soul became the emotional foundation for funk, disco, neo-soul, and contemporary R&B.
key_tracks: (Sittin On) The Dock of the Bay

name: Funk
description:
Funk strips soul music down to the rhythm and builds back up from there. The groove is the architecture: a locked-in relationship between bass and drums, with everything else either reinforcing or playing against it. James Brown invented much of the vocabulary through the mid-1960s.

Sly and the Family Stone made it psychedelic, Parliament-Funkadelic made it cosmic, and Prince made it sexual. Funk gave hip-hop its most sampled source material, and the bass lines from its golden era (1965-1985) still drive dance floors.
key_tracks: Get Up (I Feel Like Being a) Sex Machine

name: Classic Rock
description:
Classic rock is the style that dominated FM radio from the mid-1960s through the late 1970s: guitar-forward, hook-driven, built for arenas. The sound ranges from the blues-rooted work of Led Zeppelin and Cream to the melodic precision of the Eagles and the eccentricities of the Who.

It is defined as much by its audience as its sound. These were albums made to be played loud and listened to in full, and the artists expected to be taken seriously. The genre established the template for mainstream rock that held for decades.

name: Psychedelic Rock
description:
Psychedelic rock used extended song structures, studio experimentation, and deliberately disorienting sounds to mirror the experience of consciousness expansion. Backward tape, Indian drones, unconventional chord progressions, and extended improvisation all became part of the toolkit.

The Beatles Sgt. Peppers, Jimi Hendrix's Are You Experienced, and Pink Floyd's early records set the high-water marks. The genre fed directly into progressive rock, ambient music, and the late 1980s psychedelic revival.
key_tracks: Lucy in the Sky with Diamonds

name: Progressive Rock
description:
Progressive rock applied the ambition and complexity of classical composition to rock instruments. Long tracks, concept albums, odd time signatures, synthesizers, and literary or fantasy themes were the markers. It was music that wanted to be taken as seriously as Beethoven.

Yes, Genesis, King Crimson, and Emerson Lake and Palmer defined the genre in the early 1970s. Critics resented its pretension; fans loved the ambition. Prog influenced metal, art rock, and a strain of serious songwriting that never entirely went away.

name: Hard Rock
description:
Hard rock pushed the amplifier past the point of comfort and kept it there. Heavy riffs, distorted guitars, powerful drumming, and vocals that pushed into screaming were the defining characteristics. It was louder, harder, and more physical than what preceded it.

Led Zeppelin, Deep Purple, and Aerosmith drew the early boundaries. By the 1980s it had evolved into glam metal, and the heaviest end of it fed directly into heavy metal. The form never really died; it just went underground and came back louder.

name: Heavy Metal
description:
Heavy metal is built on down-tuned guitars, thundering drums, and a delivery that ranges from operatic to menacing. Black Sabbath essentially created it by treating blues structures as something dark and threatening rather than celebratory. The result was music that felt genuinely heavy.

The genre splintered throughout the 1970s and 80s into dozens of sub-genres, each pushing one element further: speed, darkness, complexity, extremity. Iron Maiden, Judas Priest, Metallica, and Slayer each defined a distinct branch of the family.

name: Punk Rock
description:
Punk was a deliberate rejection of everything that rock had become by the mid-1970s. No solos, no concept albums, no stadium shows -- just two minutes of fast, angry noise and a message that anyone could make music without waiting for permission.

The Ramones in New York and the Sex Pistols in London developed it simultaneously, though differently. Punk gave rise to post-punk, new wave, hardcore, and eventually emo. Its ethic of independence and directness shaped independent music broadly for the next five decades.

name: New Wave
description:
New wave took punk's energy and applied it to keyboards, art school ideas, and a cooler, more ironic sensibility. It was less angry than punk and more interested in style -- synthesizers, choppy rhythms, angular guitar tones, and a visual identity that matched the music.

Talking Heads, Blondie, the Police, and Devo defined different corners of the genre. It made pop music smart again and fed directly into synth-pop, post-punk, and the alternative rock that followed.
key_tracks: Don't Stand So Close to Me

name: Post-Punk
description:
Post-punk kept punk's DIY ethic and discomfort with convention but replaced speed and anger with something stranger and more experimental. Bass-heavy, angular, and often dark, it drew from funk, reggae, and art music in ways punk never did.

Joy Division, Gang of Four, Wire, and The Fall each built something distinct from the same starting point. Post-punk's aesthetic never fully went away; it resurfaces every decade in bands looking for a way to make rock music feel dangerous again.

name: Synthpop
description:
Synthpop replaced guitars with synthesizers entirely and built pop music from electronic components. Cold, precise, and often melancholy, it created an aesthetic that felt like the future -- clean lines, artificial timbres, and an emotional distance that was itself expressive.

Kraftwerk laid the foundation, and Depeche Mode, Soft Cell, and the Human League built the genre's commercial peak in the early 1980s. Synthpop fed directly into electronic pop, EBM, and the electronic music explosion of the late 1980s and 90s.

name: Electronic
description:
Electronic music treats the synthesizer and the studio itself as instruments. Its earliest practitioners -- Stockhausen, Eno, Kraftwerk -- were interested in sound as pure material, and what you could make when you removed the requirement that music be performed by humans.

The genre encompasses everything from ambient soundscapes to club music, united only by the centrality of electronic tools in creating it. Kraftwerk's influence on nearly every genre that followed is hard to overstate.

name: House
description:
House music was invented in Chicago's Black and Latino gay club scene, built on drum machines, synthesized bass lines, and soulful vocals layered over a four-on-the-floor kick drum. The Warehouse and Music Box were the venues; Frankie Knuckles and Larry Heard were the DJs who created it.

From Chicago it moved to New York, then to the UK where it ignited the acid house explosion of 1988. House has since branched into dozens of sub-genres -- deep house, tech house, progressive house, electro house -- but its original soul-influenced warmth remains the standard.

name: Deep House
description:
Deep house is slower, warmer, and more melodically complex than standard house. It incorporates jazz chords, intricate bass lines, and a moodiness that sets it apart from the harder sound of Chicago house. Larry Heard created the template with tracks that feel less like club tools and more like late-night meditations.

The genre developed its own identity in New York through the late 1980s and 90s and found a massive audience in the UK and Europe. Modern deep house has become one of the most commercially successful electronic sub-genres globally.

name: Techno
description:
Techno was created in Detroit by a small group of Black producers -- Juan Atkins, Derrick May, and Kevin Saunderson, known as the Belleville Three -- who imagined the music of a post-industrial future. Harder and more mechanical than house, it drew from Kraftwerk, funk, and science fiction.

Detroit techno reached Europe before it found a mainstream American audience, sparking the rave movement in the UK and becoming the defining sound of Berlin club culture in the 1990s. It remains one of the most influential templates in electronic music.

name: Trance
description:
Trance is built around long builds and euphoric releases, layering synthesizer arpeggios and sustained pads over a steady four-on-the-floor kick. The goal is a state of absorption -- music that draws you in and holds you there for the duration of a set.

Germany and the Netherlands developed the genre through the early 1990s, and it reached its commercial peak around 2000 with DJs like Paul van Dyk, Tiesto, and Paul Oakenfold selling out arenas worldwide. Progressive trance, psy-trance, and uplifting trance are its main descendants.

name: Drum and Bass
description:
Drum and bass pushed breakbeat rhythms to extreme speeds -- typically 160-180 BPM -- and combined them with heavy sub-bass. It grew out of the London rave scene and the jungle sound that preceded it, retaining reggae's relationship with bass weight while pushing the drums far beyond anything that had come before.

Goldie, LTJ Bukem, and Roni Size each shaped distinct corners of the genre: dark and intense, intelligent and mellow, jazz-influenced. D&B developed a dedicated international scene that has remained consistent for three decades.

name: Jungle
description:
Jungle combined fast, chopped breakbeats with reggae bass lines and a distinctly Black British aesthetic. It emerged from London's multicultural rave scene and carried the energy of sound system culture into a new electronic context.

The tempo, the bass weight, and the MCs chatting over the top all pointed toward drum and bass -- jungle was its immediate predecessor. Artists like Shy FX, General Levy, and DJ Hype defined the sound before it formalized into D&B.

name: Dubstep
description:
The original South London dubstep was slow, dark, and deeply influenced by reggae's relationship with bass and space. Producers like Skream, Benga, and Digital Mystikz made music that was as much about what wasn't happening as what was -- tension built over minimal percussion and enormous sub-bass.

The genre was exported to North America around 2010 and transformed into a very different thing: the louder, more aggressive brostep style popularized by Skrillex. The two versions share a name and little else.

name: UK Garage
description:
UK garage developed from American house music but gave it a distinctly British personality: faster at 130 BPM, with pitched-up vocal samples, syncopated rhythms, and a sheen that was both club-ready and pop-adjacent. The Kray Twins of music -- it looked sharp but had edges.

From garage came grime, dubstep, and UK funky. Artists like Craig David, MJ Cole, and Artful Dodger brought it to mainstream attention around 2000. Its rhythmic DNA runs through contemporary UK pop and electronic music.

name: Grime
description:
Grime was created in East London council estates by teenagers with pirate radio stations and cheap music software. It's fast, angular, and confrontational -- 140 BPM instrumentals built from gamelan samples and harsh digital tones, with MCs spitting lyrics about inner city life with no concession to commercial appeal.

Dizzie Rascal's Boy in da Corner in 2003 brought it critical attention, and Stormzy's rise in the mid-2010s made it a mainstream force. Grime is distinctly British in a way that few genres are -- it doesn't translate well because it's so specific to its context.

name: Ambient
description:
Brian Eno defined ambient music as music that could be either listened to or ignored -- it should be able to fill a space without demanding attention, but reward listening when you give it. The genre treats texture, atmosphere, and slow change as more important than melody or rhythm.

From Eno's four Ambient albums through Aphex Twin's Selected Ambient Works Vol. II to the current wave of ambient producers, the genre has remained a space for sonic exploration without commercial pressure. It has influenced film scoring, game audio, and meditation music broadly.

name: Hip-Hop
description:
Hip-hop started in the South Bronx as a party culture built around four elements: DJing, MCing, breakdancing, and graffiti. DJ Kool Herc extended the drum breaks from funk and soul records to give dancers more time; the MC narrated the event. From that practical invention came the most commercially dominant music of the past three decades.

Hip-hop absorbed jazz, blues samples, and electronic production tools as it evolved. It moved from New York block parties to global culture, generating sub-genres including gangsta rap, conscious hip-hop, trap, and mumble rap, each reflecting a distinct moment and geography.

name: Gangsta Rap
description:
Gangsta rap reported on life in American urban environments with a literalism that made listeners and censors uncomfortable. Ice-T and N.W.A. brought Compton and South Central Los Angeles into living rooms nationwide with lyrics about police violence, poverty, and street life told without sentimentality.

The East Coast-West Coast rivalry of the 1990s, which ended with the murders of Tupac and Biggie, defined its most commercially successful era. The genre's influence on fashion, language, and mainstream hip-hop production continues to shape the music today.

name: Trap
description:
Trap music came out of Atlanta with a specific sound: hi-hats that roll at rapid subdivisions, massive 808 kick drums, dark synthesizer atmospheres, and lyrics about the drug trade. T.I., Young Jeezy, and Gucci Mane built the genre in the mid-2000s; producers Lex Luger and Mike WiLL Made It refined the sound.

By the 2010s trap had become the dominant mode of hip-hop production, with its influence spreading into pop, electronic music, and internationally. Drake, Future, and the Migos each represent different evolutions of the Atlanta template.

name: Conscious Hip-Hop
description:
Conscious hip-hop treats the form as a vehicle for social commentary, political critique, and personal reflection. It explicitly pushes back against the materialism and violence of gangsta rap, though the two have always coexisted and borrowed from each other.

Public Enemy, A Tribe Called Quest, Common, and Kendrick Lamar represent different generations of the same impulse: using rhyme as a serious vehicle for ideas. Kendrick's To Pimp a Butterfly brought jazz-inflected conscious hip-hop to the center of mainstream conversation in 2015.

name: Jazz Rap
description:
Jazz rap samples jazz records or incorporates live jazz instrumentation as the foundation for hip-hop production. The result is richer, harmonically more complex, and often more meditative than standard hip-hop. Gang Starr and A Tribe Called Quest pioneered it in the late 1980s.

Guru's Jazzmatazz series hired jazz musicians to perform alongside rappers in real time. The genre influenced the neo-soul movement and later helped define the Kendrick Lamar and J. Cole school of production.

name: Reggae
description:
Reggae defined the sound of Jamaica to the world: the guitar or keyboard chop on the offbeat, the prominent bass, the Rastafarian spiritual content, and Bob Marley's voice delivering messages of resistance, love, and liberation. It grew directly from ska and rocksteady in the late 1960s.

Regge's influence on sound system culture, dub, dancehall, and hip-hop gives it a reach far beyond its small island origin. The bass weight of reggae runs through enormous amounts of electronic music, from jungle to dubstep.

name: Ska
description:
Ska was Jamaica's first indigenous pop genre, combining American rhythm and blues with local mento folk music and played at a fast pace with the guitar or keyboard hitting the offbeat. It was party music before it was anything else, driving the dance halls of Kingston through the early 1960s.

Ska went through three distinct waves: the original Jamaican sound, the UK two-tone ska revival of the late 1970s (The Specials, Madness), and the American third wave of the 1990s (Sublime, No Doubt). Each generation found something different in it.

name: Mento
description:
Mento is Jamaica's oldest folk music tradition, predating ska by two decades. It features acoustic guitar, banjo, hand drums, and the rhumba box -- a large thumb piano that provides the bass. The songs are often humorous and deal with everyday Jamaican life.

Mento was the music of rural Jamaica and tourist hotels before ska took over. Its influence on ska and reggae is foundational, particularly its rhythmic feel and its irreverence.

name: Dub
description:
Dub deconstructed reggae records in the studio, using the mixing board as an instrument. Engineers like King Tubby and Lee Scratch Perry would strip tracks down to bass and drums, then dramatically add and remove elements, apply extreme echo and reverb, and create entirely new compositions from existing recordings.

Dub invented the idea of the remix before the word existed. Its approach to the studio as instrument directly influenced hip-hop production, electronic music, and post-punk. The sub-bass frequencies of dub became the template for bass-heavy electronic music.

name: Dancehall
description:
Dancehall shifted reggae away from the political content of roots music toward the dance floor. Digital rhythms replaced live bands, the tempo increased, and the lyrical focus turned to dancing, romance, and street life. It emerged as a distinct form in the late 1970s with artists like Yellowman and Eek-A-Mouse.

Dancehall influenced UK garage, grime, and soca, and its rhythm patterns appear in modern pop production globally. Vybz Kartel and Popcaan represent its contemporary form.

name: Disco
description:
Disco was the sound of liberation -- of Black, Latino, and gay communities in New York finding a space to be themselves at full volume. Four-on-the-floor kick drums, string arrangements, falsetto vocals, and the continuous mix pioneered by DJs created a new kind of social space: the club as its own world.

The backlash (Disco Sucks) was partly about music and largely about the communities that made it. When disco was commercially buried in 1979, the underground scenes that created it kept going and invented house music. Everything that happened in clubs afterward traces back here.

name: Pop
description:
Pop music is defined by its accessibility: it wants to be heard by as many people as possible and is shaped by that ambition. Short songs, singable hooks, verse-chorus structures, and professional production are its tools. It absorbs from every other genre and reflects whatever the mainstream is willing to accept.

The Beatles industrialized it in the 1960s; Michael Jackson perfected it in the 1980s; streaming algorithms have since reshaped what it sounds like. Pop is less a genre than a function -- the commercial surface through which other music reaches large audiences.

name: Indie Rock
description:
Indie rock defined itself against the major label system before it described a sound. Independent distribution, DIY recording, and an aversion to commercial calculation were its first characteristics. The sound varied enormously -- from the quiet intensity of Beat Happening to the noise of Sonic Youth.

By the 2000s indie rock had become its own mainstream, with bands like The Strokes, Arctic Monkeys, and Arcade Fire reaching large audiences while maintaining the aesthetic of independence. The term lost some precision as it gained popularity, but the attitude survived.

name: Alternative Rock
description:
Alternative rock was a catch-all term for guitar music that didn't fit into mainstream radio formats in the 1980s, and then a genuine genre movement once Nirvana proved it could sell millions of records. It covers enormous sonic territory -- from the jangle-pop of R.E.M. to the noise of Dinosaur Jr.

Grunge was its commercial peak in the early 1990s. After Kurt Cobain's death the genre fragmented into post-grunge, nu-metal, emo, and math rock. Alternative's main legacy is demonstrating that rock music made outside the mainstream could reach the mainstream without changing.

name: Grunge
description:
Grunge came from Seattle and combined the heaviness of metal with the attitude of punk and a melodic sense closer to classic rock than either. Distorted guitars, quiet-loud dynamics, and a deliberate rejection of polish defined the sound. The flannel shirts and lack of pretense were part of the aesthetic.

Nirvana's Nevermind in 1991 turned a regional underground into a global phenomenon. Pearl Jam, Soundgarden, and Alice in Chains each had distinct takes on the same Seattle sensibility. Grunge ended more than it began -- its commercial peak also brought about the conditions that made it unsustainable.

name: Emo
description:
Emo started as an extension of hardcore punk that allowed for more emotional range and melodic complexity. The name came from the community's description of the music as emotional hardcore. Rites of Spring and Embrace in Washington D.C. created the template in the mid-1980s.

The 2000s saw a second wave -- My Chemical Romance, Fall Out Boy, Paramore -- that was more polished and reached a massive teenage audience. Emo became associated with a specific visual identity (black hair, eyeliner) that sometimes overshadowed the music's real emotional ambition.

name: Shoegaze
description:
Shoegaze musicians played with their heads down, eyes fixed on their effects pedals -- hence the name, coined dismissively by the British music press. The sound submerged vocals under walls of guitar distortion and reverb, creating music where texture and atmosphere mattered more than traditional song elements.

My Bloody Valentine's Loveless is the defining document. Slowdive, Ride, and Lush each refined aspects of the formula. Shoegaze was critically neglected when it happened and has since become enormously influential on bedroom pop, dream pop, and guitar music broadly.

name: Thrash Metal
description:
Thrash metal pushed heavy metal to extreme speeds while incorporating the aggression and DIY ethic of punk. The guitars down-tuned and syncopated, the drumming became a barrage, and the vocals raged. The Big Four -- Metallica, Slayer, Megadeth, and Anthrax -- defined the genre in the early 1980s.

Thrash influenced death metal, groove metal, and crossover thrash. Metallica's Black Album in 1991 brought the sound to a mainstream audience, though purists argued it had softened too much to qualify.

name: Death Metal
description:
Death metal took thrash metal's speed and aggression and made them more extreme in every dimension. Vocals became guttural roars rather than screams, guitar tunings dropped lower, song structures became more complex, and the lyrical content dealt with death, violence, and the grotesque.

Florid and Possessed were the genre's American originators; Swedish death metal from Gothenburg gave it a melodic dimension in the early 1990s. Death metal developed its own global underground that has remained consistent and self-sustaining for four decades.

name: Black Metal
description:
Black metal emerged from a specific rejection of everything mainstream, including mainstream heavy metal. Intentionally lo-fi production, tremolo guitar riffs, blast beat drumming, and shrieks rather than roars defined the sound. The early Norwegian scene -- Mayhem, Burzum, Darkthrone -- created an atmosphere of genuine menace that was only partly performance.

The genre's cultural scandals (church burnings, murders within the scene) gave it an extreme notoriety. Musically it has evolved toward atmospheric black metal, post-black metal, and experimental territory, absorbing influences from ambient to jazz.

name: Afrobeats
description:
Afrobeats (with the s) is distinct from Fela Kuti's Afrobeat -- it is the Nigerian pop sound of the 21st century, blending Yoruba musical traditions with hip-hop production, dancehall rhythms, and the energy of Lagos. It's music for dancing, with melody and rhythm both elevated.

Burna Boy, Wizkid, and Davido brought it to global audiences through the 2010s. The genre is now one of the most commercially significant in the world, with its rhythms appearing in mainstream pop productions everywhere.

name: Afrobeat
description:
Afrobeat is the invention of Fela Kuti, who combined Yoruba musical traditions, American jazz, and James Brown's funk into something political and relentless. The compositions are long and hypnotic, built on interlocking horn arrangements and percussion, with Fela's vocals delivering satirical attacks on Nigerian corruption.

Fela was arrested, beaten, and had his commune burned down by the government. His music documented the cost. His son Femi and grandson Made have continued the tradition, but the original is incomparable.

name: K-Pop
description:
K-Pop is a highly produced South Korean pop music industry built around groups assembled, trained, and managed by entertainment companies. The music combines Western pop structures with hip-hop, electronic production, and elaborate choreography into a total package designed for global consumption.

BTS and BLACKPINK brought it to Western mainstream attention through the 2010s, but the genre had already built a massive pan-Asian fanbase. K-Pop's model of fandom -- the parasocial intensity, the merchandise, the fan communities -- has influenced how other genres market themselves.

name: Bossa Nova
description:
Bossa nova emerged in the beach neighborhoods of Rio de Janeiro in the late 1950s as a refined, intimate response to samba. Joao Gilberto's quiet guitar style, Tom Jobim's sophisticated harmonies, and Vinicius de Moraes's poetry combined into something that felt like cool air in a humid room.

Girl from Ipanema is the best-known example but represents only one dimension of the genre. Stan Getz's American version brought it to global audiences in the early 1960s. Bossa nova's influence on jazz, ambient music, and cocktail culture has been continuous.

name: Samba
description:
Samba is the musical heart of Brazilian culture, developed in Rio de Janeiro by communities with African roots in the early 20th century. The rhythmic interlocking of percussion instruments -- the surdo bass drum, tamborim, pandeiro, and cuica -- creates a groove that is unmistakably Brazilian.

Carnival samba, samba-cancao, and samba-pagode are the main variations. Samba became a symbol of Brazilian national identity and fed directly into bossa nova and MPB (Brazilian Popular Music).

name: Flamenco
description:
Flamenco is the art music of the Romani people of Andalusia, Spain, combining guitar, song (cante), and dance (baile) into an intensely expressive form. The guitar technique -- rasgueado strumming, falsetas, and complex rhythmic cycles called palos -- is among the most technically demanding in any folk tradition.

The emotion of flamenco is called duende -- a quality of authentic feeling that cannot be faked. Paco de Lucia brought its guitar tradition to international audiences, and the genre has influenced jazz, classical guitar, and world music broadly.

name: Neo-Soul
description:
Neo-soul updated classic soul's warmth and emotional directness with hip-hop production sensibilities and a more introspective lyrical approach. It rejected the slick production of contemporary R&B in favor of live instrumentation, vinyl crackle, and a vintage aesthetic that honored Marvin Gaye and Curtis Mayfield.

D'Angelo's Brown Sugar in 1995 defined the sound; Lauryn Hill's Miseducation of Lauryn Hill and Erykah Badu's Baduizm completed the first wave. The genre influenced everything from Amy Winehouse to Frank Ocean to the current wave of bedroom R&B.

name: Motown
description:
Motown was both a record label and a genre -- the first Black-owned company to achieve consistent mainstream pop crossover success. Berry Gordy Jr. in Detroit applied assembly-line production principles to soul music, creating a house style with distinctive bass lines, string arrangements, and call-and-response vocals.

The Supremes, The Temptations, Marvin Gaye, Stevie Wonder, and The Jackson 5 all came from the Motown stable. The sound was deliberately designed to appeal across racial lines without sacrificing its Black identity -- a commercial and artistic achievement that still sounds effortless.

name: Reggaeton
description:
Reggaeton built its signature dembow rhythm from Jamaican dancehall and combined it with Latin hip-hop and urban production. The sound developed in Puerto Rico through the 1990s before exploding globally in the mid-2000s with Daddy Yankee's Gasolina.

Bad Bunny, J Balvin, and Maluma have brought it to the center of global pop, making it one of the most commercially dominant genres in the world. Its influence on mainstream pop production, from the dembow beat pattern to Spanish-language crossover hits, is now pervasive.

name: Latin Pop
description:
Latin pop blends the production values of international pop with Spanish and Portuguese language music traditions. The genre encompasses an enormous range of sounds -- from the romantica ballads of the 1980s to the Latin urban explosion of the late 1990s that brought Ricky Martin, Jennifer Lopez, and Marc Anthony to global mainstream audiences.

Shakira, Enrique Iglesias, and later artists like Rosalia and Karol G represent different eras and approaches within the broad category. The genre has consistently crossed language barriers by leading with melody and rhythm.

name: Intelligent Dance Music
description:
Intelligent Dance Music -- the term came from a Warp Records compilation and was almost immediately resented by the artists it described -- is electronic music that prioritizes complexity and textural interest over functionality on the dance floor. Irregular rhythms, intricate sound design, and emotional range distinguish it from harder club music.

Aphex Twin, Boards of Canada, Autechre, and Squarepusher defined the Warp Records sound that anchored the genre. IDM's influence on experimental electronic music, film soundtracks, and even classical composition has been substantial.

name: Progressive House
description:
Progressive house applies trance music's long builds and emotional arcs to the four-on-the-floor structures of house. The music moves slowly, adding and subtracting elements over extended periods, and the peak of a progressive house track can feel genuinely cathartic after a careful 8-minute approach.

Sasha, Digweed, and Boy George (the DJ, not the singer) defined the British version in the 1990s. Daft Punk's early work touched on the genre, and Eric Prydz has been its most consistent commercial practitioner since.

name: New Jack Swing
description:
New jack swing was producer Teddy Riley's synthesis of R&B and hip-hop: the smooth vocals and radio-friendly production of soul combined with drum machine beats and hip-hop production techniques. Bobby Brown, Keith Sweat, and Guy defined the genre in the late 1980s.

The sound bridged the gap between old-school soul and contemporary R&B and influenced the entire trajectory of Black pop through the 1990s. Michael Jackson and Janet Jackson both incorporated its elements into their 1990s work.

name: Glam Rock
description:
Glam rock made spectacle the point. Marc Bolan, David Bowie, and Gary Glitter took rock and roll and covered it in platform boots, makeup, and sequins, turning performance into theater and sexuality into something deliberately ambiguous. The music was catchy, the image was provocative.

Glam's gender-bending aesthetics were genuinely transgressive in the early 1970s. Bowie's Ziggy Stardust remains its defining statement. The genre fed directly into heavy metal (the glam/hair metal of the 1980s), punk (as a reaction), and the new romantic movement.

name: Indie Pop
description:
Indie pop emphasized melody and sweetness over the noise and aggression of indie rock. The jangly guitar of Scottish bands like Orange Juice, Aztec Camera, and later Belle and Sebastian gave it a specific sound: clean, bright, a little melancholy, and stubbornly uncommercial in its presentation.

The C86 cassette compilation defined the British version; American indie pop has ranged from the Magnetic Fields to Vampire Weekend. The genre prioritizes songwriting craft over sonic extremity.
key_tracks: The Boy With the Arab Strap

name: Dream Pop
description:
Dream pop layers atmospheric production over melodic songwriting, creating music that feels suspended and immersive. Reverb-drenched guitars, breathy vocals, and slow tempos are the tools. Cocteau Twins invented most of the vocabulary; Beach House has been its most consistent modern practitioner.

Dream pop sits between shoegaze's noise and ambient's formlessness -- it has songs, melodies you can follow, but the texture is as important as any element. The genre has influenced bedroom pop, indie folk, and a strain of R&B production.

name: Math Rock
description:
Math rock applies the rhythmic complexity of progressive rock to the DIY ethic and sonic abrasion of indie rock. Irregular time signatures, interlocking guitar lines, and the absence of conventional rhythm guitar distinguish it. The music is technically demanding and resists casual listening.

Slint's Spiderland is often cited as the foundational document. Shellac, Tortoise, and Don Caballero built on it in the 1990s. Math rock has remained a consistent underground genre, influential on post-rock and instrumental music broadly.

name: Post-Rock
description:
Post-rock uses rock instrumentation to build music that has little to do with rock conventions. There are rarely vocals, rarely hooks in the traditional sense -- instead long pieces that build from quiet passages to massive climaxes through patience and dynamics. The guitar acts more like an orchestral instrument than a rock guitar.

Talk Talk's Spirit of Eden pointed the direction; Godspeed You! Black Emperor and Explosions in the Sky defined the genre's emotional range. Post-rock is scoring music by temperament -- its influence on film soundtracks has been enormous.

name: Happy Hardcore
description:
Happy hardcore is drum and bass at 165-185 BPM pitched up into pure euphoria: sped-up piano riffs, chipmunk vocals, and an intensity that borders on absurdity. It was the sound of Saturday night rave culture at its most committed, music that demanded maximum energy and gave it back.

The genre was critically dismissed but culturally significant -- it captured the peak emotional experience of British rave culture in the early 1990s. Smiley Culture and producers like Hixxy and Sharkey defined it.

name: Reggaeton
description:
Reggaeton built its signature dembow rhythm from Jamaican dancehall and combined it with Latin hip-hop and urban production. The sound developed in Puerto Rico through the 1990s before exploding globally in the mid-2000s with Daddy Yankee's Gasolina.

Bad Bunny, J Balvin, and Maluma have brought it to the center of global pop, making it one of the most commercially dominant genres in the world. Its influence on mainstream pop production, from the dembow beat pattern to Spanish-language crossover hits, is now pervasive.

name: Cumbia
description:
Cumbia is Colombia's most internationally recognized music, combining Indigenous percussion, African rhythms, and Spanish melodic elements into a genre built for dancing. The clave pattern, accordion or gaita flute, and call-and-response singing are its essentials.

Cumbia spread through Latin America in the mid-20th century and took on distinct regional flavors in Mexico, Peru, and Argentina. Its influence on salsa, norteño, and cumbia villera is extensive.

name: Salsa
description:
Salsa developed in New York City among Puerto Rican and Cuban communities who combined son cubano, jazz, and other Caribbean rhythms into something that belonged specifically to the Latin urban experience. Fania Records in New York was the center of it in the 1970s.

Celia Cruz and Hector Lavoe defined the classic era. Salsa romantica softened the sound in the 1980s, and the genre has continued to evolve while maintaining its rhythmic foundations and social function as dance music.

name: Amapiano
description:
Amapiano emerged from the townships of South Africa as a slow-tempo house variant built around the log drum -- a synth bass that produces a distinctive, bouncy low-end pattern. Combined with jazzy piano chords and layered percussion, it creates something hypnotic and uniquely South African.

The genre spread globally through social media and TikTok from around 2020, influencing UK and Nigerian music and becoming one of the most significant new African genres in decades. DJ Maphorisa, Kabza De Small, and Focalistic are its leading figures.

name: Highlife
description:
Highlife developed in Ghana and Nigeria in the 1920s as music for the urban elite -- hence the name. It combined Western brass band arrangements and jazz harmonies with African rhythms and local languages, creating a sophisticiated dance music that spread across West Africa.

Highlife was the dominant urban music of West Africa through the 1950s and 60s before being eclipsed by afrobeat and jùjú. Its melodic sensibility influenced afrobeats and remains a foundational reference in West African popular music.

name: Indian Classical
description:
Indian classical music encompasses two distinct traditions: Hindustani from the north and Carnatic from the south. Both are built around ragas -- melodic frameworks with specific ascending and descending patterns, emotional associations, and times of day or seasons for performance -- and talas, which are rhythmic cycles.

The tradition is primarily improvised within strict formal constraints, and a performance can last hours as a musician explores a single raga. Indian classical music influenced jazz (through John Coltrane), rock (through George Harrison and the Beatles), and electronic music broadly through its relationship with drone and repetition.

name: Bebop
description:
Bebop was jazz's first self-conscious art music movement, created by musicians who wanted to play music that was too complex to be easily copied or danced to. Charlie Parker and Dizzy Gillespie developed the vocabulary: fast tempos, altered chords, complex melodic lines, and improvisation that required genuine technical mastery.

Bebop separated jazz from its popular function and established it as a music for listening rather than dancing. It influenced every subsequent jazz style and introduced a conception of improvisation as spontaneous composition that spread far beyond the genre.

name: Modal Jazz
description:
Modal jazz replaced the rapid chord changes of bebop with static or slowly changing harmonic fields -- modes rather than chord progressions -- giving soloists more space to develop melodic ideas. Miles Davis's Kind of Blue in 1959 is the definitive document, and it remains the best-selling jazz album ever made.

The shift from chords to modes had a profound effect on jazz and beyond. It opened jazz to Indian music, influenced rock guitarists, and created a meditative quality that distinguished it from the busier language of bebop.

name: Free Jazz
description:
Free jazz abandoned the harmonic, rhythmic, and structural conventions that had defined jazz since its earliest form. Ornette Coleman's 1960 album Free Jazz: A Collective Improvisation gave the movement its name. The result was music of startling intensity and no reliable signposts for listeners.

Ayler, Cecil Taylor, and Pharoah Sanders each took the freedom in different directions. Free jazz permanently expanded what jazz could be and influenced noise music, experimental classical composition, and a strain of avant-garde rock.

name: Jazz Fusion
description:
Jazz fusion brought electric instruments, rock rhythms, and studio effects into jazz, creating something that had the improvisational sophistication of jazz and the energy of rock. Miles Davis's Bitches Brew in 1970 defined the template; Weather Report, Mahavishnu Orchestra, and Return to Forever each developed distinct versions.

Fusion was commercially successful, critically contested (purists felt it diluted jazz), and musically influential. Its hybrid approach anticipated how genres would cross-pollinate for the rest of the century.
